comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is  here, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions involving the evocation of spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book above was my thirty-ninth published book and my fifth published novel, and this one -- unlike the one we discussed last week -- I intended to write. Retrotopia began life as a series of posts on the blog I ran then, The Archdruid Report. I wanted to take the themes I was developing for two nonfiction books, After Progress and The Retro Future, and use fiction as a way of making them vivid and understandable. So I sent Peter Carr, a high-ranking political adviser to the newly elected president of one of several post-US republics, across the border into a neighboring republic and a confrontation with a nation that had leapt ahead of everyone else by going back to the past.

Writing it was an interesting experience; as with my earlier venture along the same lines, Star's Reach, some of my readers literally couldn't think the thoughts the story was trying to get them to think, and kept defaulting to the approved notions of the modern cult of progress. Nonetheless the book, once published, turned out to be my most popular work of fiction. It's currently out of print but will be back in print this coming November; you can preorder a copy here if you live in the US and here if you live elsewhere.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed, and no further comments will be put through. See you next week!***

comments.title: Cardinals, fig tree and Thor
comments.content:
Happy Magic Monday, JMG,
I have been reading The Occult Philosophy Workbook since shortly after the new year and am throughly enjoying it, but It’s been turtle speed all the way. I’ve reached The Plant Kingdom this past week and wanted to share some random thoughts.

Last Monday I was holding my dog outside the vet’s office, in the distance, a cardinal was signing. They are my favorite birds and as I listened to its distinctive call, I thought how, for me, it was like heaven on earth. Later that day, I saw your comment about birds being angels to the trees they visit, and squirrels being little cupids. It made me laugh and has settled into my subconscious.

On July 13th, the fig tree in my backyard fell over after heavy rains. Not knowing what we were doing when we planted it some ten years ago, it lacked a full diameter to grow strong roots all around. It was suggested that we let it go. To say I was crestfallen would be an understatement. With a heavy heart, I accepted its fate. The day before the arborist came, I put both hands on its trunk and prayed a deeply felt thanks and told the tree spirit it was time to go. Last week, my beloved fig tree returned to me in a dream. It was ten times its previous size, I sat perched in one of its branches. Despite the dizzying height, I felt no fear, in fact, more than anything, I felt comfort. On a nearby branch, I noticed a green chameleon with no eyes. We communicated in a way that’s hard to describe. I poked and played with it, its movements were slow at first but then it leaped into the sky as if it was made of liquid, transforming from within with a radiant light, it turned into a flying squirrel. Once its transformation was complete, it glided to the distant world below. I woke up shortly thereafter, with an incredibly warm feeling. Dreams with large trees have consistently been the best and most memorable dreams I have ever had.

Despite some protests, I kept a small piece of the fig tree trunk. When I saw your post about wooden statues of Celtic deities and the responses posted, I was glad that I did and have been thinking about what I could carve ever since. But beyond that inspiration, I also made an impulse buy over at Ancient Gods.

Thor is on his way.

Not sure what I’m going to do with him other than put him in a prominent place where I work every day so when I looks away from my computer, we can see each other eye to eye.

Any additional thoughts or suggestions are welcome.

replies.title: Re: Cardinals, fig tree and Thor
replies.content: Thank you for this! Good to know my random comments have been useful -- and that sounds like a truly wonderful dream.

replies.title: Re: Cardinals, fig tree and Thor
replies.content:
Hi,
You can take a couple of cuttings from the fig and pot them up. Some of them should root and the tree would live on.

I think it is marvelous that your tree came back to say goodbye to you. Some of my friends and animal have done that with me, but never a tree.
Hugs from Maxine

replies.title: Re: Cardinals, fig tree and Thor
replies.content:
Wow, it is not often that a full blown deity reveals its potency through a dream! Some god seems to have been quite deeply moved by your caring actions.

Although that’s not a god I have ever encountered before, so I cannot help you with its identification, it appears to have some deep connection with trees and possibly specifically with fig trees. Did you know that the banyan fig trees planted at Hindu temples are treated as natural dwellings for divine presence? In Indonesia they get encircled in poleng cloth to keep the sacred trees' potent spirits safely contained within them.

That god brought you up to its own dizzying height to experience its changeable nature, starting out with a simple metaphor you would understand: a chameleon. A blind one, no less, since any being who expresses itself through changing images is unlikely to let itself be easily distracted by others' surface appearances. Some divinity who focuses inside onto objects' true natures has turned its attention towards you, and found you worthy of its contemplation. That is quite the letter of recommendation!

Have you considered using some of your tree's wood to carve a totem to that god, who so willingly accepted the spirit of the fig tree you handed over into its care? A blind chameleon, liquidly transforming into a flying squirrel, is a magical image worthy of lasting contemplation. More importantly, that god may well appreciate your providing a convenient vehicle for it to continue its divine contemplation of you in return.

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: Cardinals, fig tree and Thor
replies.content:
Thank you, Christophe.

My fig tree was a large presence in a small yard. I had advocated for it year after year to the other family member who thought it should go. With each passing year, I loved it more. Sitting under it, on the stairs that led to the patio was a favorite moment of Zen. To anyone other than the great visitors here, my conversations with it probably seemed nutty.

Its fall was a real blow.

I had no idea about banyan fig trees but now that I know, I think I would fit right in at those Indonesian temples. The trunk I kept is drying out at the top, the gaps of which are making a five pointed star. Everything about the tree was magical to me. I will definitely give care and consideration to what to the trunk could become. And a big thank you about the letter of recommendation. I'll take all the help I can get.

replies.content:
Not medical advice, and not occult lore, and very hard to answer without knowing more specifics, but roughly speaking there is a difference between a type of insomnia where you have trouble falling asleep, versus an insomnia where you have trouble staying asleep. What's helped for me, based on my knowledge and experience of herbalism:

- For falling asleep due to an overly active mind: valerian tea or tincture (I'm not one of the 5%-10% of people who get stimulated rather than sedated by valerian), passion flower tea or tincture, and california poppy tincture.
- For staying asleep: motherwort tincture.

Generally, what's also helped in conjunction with the above:
- Addressing sources of any stress (easier said than done, and obviously depends on specifics)
- Not eating 2-3 hours before bedtime (especially heavy meals)
- Exercising regularly to work off excess energy, which would otherwise end up taking the form of too much mental activity at night.
- Daily magnesium capsules as a general relaxant (a bottle is expensive, but lasts me for several months).

Might be worth seeing a herbalist in your area to get properly assessed, because herbs actually can provide a lot of help for sleep issues.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Decidedly non-occult, a few things to add to this, especially useful for sleep-onset insomnia (can't fall asleep when you go to bed):

- If you enjoy caffeinated beverages, consider stopping their intake much earlier than seems necessary, like no more caffeine after 1:00 or 2:00 pm. I thought I had sleep-onset insomnia for years, then read that some folks are slow metabolizers of caffeine - they tend not to feel as strong of a "rush" when they have caffeine, but the effects linger more subtly for longer. Looks like I'm one of those, because once I made a rule to not have caffeine after the early afternoon about 80-90% of my issues went away.
- "Sleep hygiene" is a term you might do a websearch on to get some ideas, but the basics are to train your subconscious that your bed is for sleeping and intimacy and nothing else, and to create the right conditions for those. So, no checking phones in bed, no sitting in bed to do anything more stimulating than reading for enjoyment, and so forth. Make sure your room is dark, and if you can't do that, a sleep mask works wonders. Most folks fall asleep more easily in cooler temperatures than warm, so consider ways to cool down your room after dark. Avoiding bright lights, especially lights with a lot of blue in them, can help as well (backlit screens like computers, phones, and tablets, are especially bad for this, but programs like f.luxs or Mac's built-in "night shift" can help somewhat).
- Laying in bed is a great time to practice relaxation methods, whether breathing, tensing and releasing muscles, visualization, or whatever. If you fall asleep in the middle, hooray! If not, well, you still got more relaxed for later
- Lastly, if you find yourself truly wired, consider getting up and doing something else that is quiet and not overly stimulating - read for a bit with a fairly dim light, get a drink of water, step outside for some fresh air, that sort of thing. Then come back ready to sleep, if you can

If you struggle with waking up in the night rather than falling asleep, most of these tips ought to help some, but maybe not as directly. Either way, good luck!

Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This is just my own thoughts.

For actually falling asleep, for some reason I find that concentrating on the base of my spine works. Maybe moving energy down to lower centres does the trick, I don't know, but it works for me.

Waking in the night used to worry me until I read about the history of sleep and how, in pre-industrial times, humans used to have a 'first sleep' and a 'second sleep', getting up for a while between the two. Once I'd taken that in, sleep came more easily. Other mammals do similar and most have several short periods of sleep over the day and night - for example, the sheep and cows next to where I work invariably all sit down for a nap around 1pm. Birds are great nappers too. Before industrialisation, it was just normal to go to bed when it got dark, sleep for a while, get up for an hour or so in the night, and then have another sleep until dawn. In the scheme of things, it's not so long since this way of sleeping was the norm and we haven't had long to adapt to the modern so-called ideal of eight hours of uninterrupted sleep, so it's not surprising that a lot of people can't do it. Thinking about it, everyone sleeping all night doesn't have any evolutionary advantages and at one time would have put you at risk of predation.

Getting enough sleep can be an issue if you're inclined towards biphasic sleep, so I organise my day to do so, going to bed early enough that waking in the night isn't a problem. A big plus is waking naturally and not needing an alarm clock.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What worked for me was getting rid of electronic lights in my bedroom, and make sure to go to bed around sunset whenever possible. This was not just the obvious lights, but also the alarm clock; and installing curtains to block enough of the light from the streetlight near my house. Within a week of this I was sleeping better than I could remember.

replies.title: (no subject)

Here's my purely personal experience:

I had sleep problems for a year which had got to the point where the anxiety about not sleeping was a major part of why I couldn't sleep. One Saturday, I prayed to Saturn for help.

A few days later, it occurred to me that I could use affirmations to deal with the mental part of the insomnia, even if it was partly physical. It was my mind that was focusing on all the tiny noises rather than screening them out, right? So I crafted an affirmation, "I sleep soundly through the night." Every time I couldn't sleep after that, I repeated my affirmation - it gave me something to do while I was awake. The anxiety subsided almost immediately, and the insomnia was mostly gone within a month.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
JMG,

Regarding the Druid Wand, Cauldron, and Crane Bag enchantments in the DMH:

(1) The white, blue, and green colour-breathing assigned to each of these workings: are they an essential component of the structure of each of those three rituals, or is that a variable that changes based on intention? For example, if I’m using the Druid Wand / Nwyfre ritual to enchant something other than a wand, will the colour breathing portion of the ritual always include white light no matter what I’m enchanting or why?

(2) It seems to me that each of those rituals falls under one of three categories of enchantment: active, passive, and both active and passive.

I can think of things to enchant with the active category, such as talismans.

But I can’t think of a way to use the other two enchantments outside of the Cauldron and Crane Bag tools.

Could you point my creativity in the right direction?

(3) If pathworking a few that ends at the Central Grove, do we trace any sort pentagram after arriving? And is there a Druid Revival god we should invoke there?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) They're a variable that changes depending on intention and symbolism. Those three colors correspond to the three Druid elements, which are used to charge the three working tools. You can use any color -- for example, the Ogham colors, if you want to enchant something with the energies of an Ogham few.

2) It's quite simple. Magic is not always active; sometimes it is receptive. For example, if you meant to consecrate a scrying mirror, the cauldron working is suitable. Similarly, you can use the crane bag working for anything that needs to be enchanted but has neither an active nor a receptive function -- for example, certain working tools, such as a knife for harvesting herbs, are well consecrated that way.

3) You can trace the three rays of light instead, and the deity is Ced the Earth Mother.

1) It's not equivalent, but it's still an issue; if you made a promise, you need to keep it, and you will keep it -- in this life or another. That doesn't mean you have to get back in touch with the person, but I recommend journaling on the problem between you until you can wholeheartedly forgive him, and ask the Christian god to bless him.

2) Incompetent cursing has two karmic dimensions -- the karma of cursing, and the karma of incompetence. The more space you give in your life to hatred, anger, and malice, whether you express it outwardly or not, the more hateful, angry, and malicious you become, and the more people treat you the way they treat hateful, angry, and malicious people -- i.e., not well. Equally, the more tolerant you are of your own incompetence, the more incompetent you become, and that spreads from one area (such as magic) to everything else in life. So these people are setting themselves up for a really miserable future, in which they get to stew in their own nastiness while sinking further and further into failure. The good news is that eventually they'll be miserable enough to do something else.

3) I don't recommend doing that. You're increasing the possibility that you or the people you care about will become the victims of violence.

replies.title: Friendship
replies.content: With respect to your first question, there is a reflection of your dilemma in the Pythagorean writings (Hierocles' commentary on the Golden Verses). Essentially, it says you can't abandon a friend lightly, but only if he or she has departed from virtue. There is a corollary in the Akousma of Pythagoras: "Don't give your right hand to just anyone", which means be careful with who you enter into the bonds of friendship, because they are bonds indeed! Swearing an oath to someone as your "Brother in Christ" sounds like exactly the degree of friendship that was being thought of in the earlier philosophy. So it would seem that departing from that friendship would hinge on the other's departure from the path of virtue. What "virtue" means in this context requires some real consideration. Too, it isn't necessary that a departure from relationship necessarily means a departure from friendship. It could just be viewed as "quarantine". I just recovered from my second bout of COVID. Just because I kept my distance from my wife and slept on an air mattress in another room didn't mean the marriage was over...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: To JMG and the commentariat: what is your opinion of The Origin of Consciousness in the Breakdown of the Bicameral Mind?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: To my mind, it's a very, very mixed bag. What I found most useful about it is Jaynes' willingness to consider the possibility that people in past ages had states of consciousness radically different from our own, and his refreshing openness to testimony about divine voices and the like. On the other hand, his brain science is far too simplistic, as there are something like five semi-separate brain/nervous system elements -- the right cerebral hemisphere, the left cerebral hemispheres, the limbic system, the R-complex (brain stem and "reptilian" brain), and the solar plexus (the "abdominal brain," as it was once called) and the rest of the sympathetic nervous system. A neuropsychology, to be valid, would have to include all those.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Building on JMG's response, I found it fascinating and provocative when I stumbled onto it by way of Neal Stephenson's fiction, where it plays a prominent role in some of his earlier books (notably as a major plot driver in The Big U and Snowcrash, with a few echoes in Diamond Age. If nothing else, it's a marvelous tool for entertaining a hard-to-get-your-head-around hypothesis (as described by Eric S. Raymond here: http://esr.ibiblio.org/?p=4893). If you find the right-brain/left-brain stuff interesting, Ian McGilchrist has written quite a bit on it, most recently and notably in his hulking two-volume The Matter With Things (which I have, but haven't read yet). I suspect that much of the weakness in his analysis come from a combination of modernity bias and insisting on purely materialist explanations for everything he discusses - the voices must only be auditory hallucinations representing the thoughts of the other hemisphere, "consciousness" must only mean an internal monologue associated with the "self," and so forth. Still, as JMG says, his willingness to say "no, really, guys, read the Iliad and the Old Testament and actually pay attention to what they're saying. Maybe they're not just metaphors!" is really interesting.

Cheers,
Jeff

comments.title: Jason Miller
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

What do you think of Jason Miller and his writings?

I'm not asking for a detailed breakdown and analysis - more just an indication of where he is on the spectrum between "total charlatan - stay away" on one end and "solid, respected practitioner whose opinions happen to be different from JMG" on the other.

I have no ritual magic experience (only meditation) but have read MM here for years and read several of your books and stuff you've recommended (Dion Fortune etc).

I picked up one of Miller's books about summoning spirits (and then another one about protection magic) and am just kind of blown away by the different vibe and attitude to what I see around here.

NOTE: I am not asking a question about how to do evocation of spirits - more just a vibe check on the author (that just happens to be the first Miller book I picked up and developed an impression of him, and I have since got his Protection Magic book too, where the attitude is very similar).

The vibe here at Ecosophia seems to be more "study, learn the basics and do basics and eventually build up to rituals and advanced stuff" but his seems to be "just jump in and start doing rituals and calling spirits and handle things as you go along".

I know everyone has a different risk tolerance and different style but the way he's so casual about summoning demons and really powerful spirits just makes me feel nervous - he does say "don't do this if you're uncomfortable" but there's literally summonings praising Lucifer and summoning him to aid you in the book with essentially no guardrails (he says to check out his other book on protection magic) and no building up from the basics (other than a brief reference saying you should meditate).

Plus the reference to having a long term friendly relationship over decades with the demon Astaroth and how it's very unfriendly and aggressive to summon demons in the name of the Divine and force them to do so stuff, and it's much better to issue a friendly invitation and work with them as a team etc. Plus I don't understand how one can be familiar enough with so many different traditions of magic to be able to competently summon Tibetan Buddhist spirits, Loa spirits, traditional Western magic spirits and the like.

Like I said - everyone has a different style and there's nothing wrong with that and I'm enjoying his books - heis a very engaging writer and clearly has had a lot of experience and success with his approach but as someone whose background is in reading your stuff about magic, this whole approach strikes me as playing really fast and loose with some very dangerous entities and situations with a very high potential for blowback.

replies.title: Re: Jason Miller
replies.content: I'm not a fan of his work at all, largely for the reasons you've mentioned. I don't think he's a charlatan -- he clearly believes in what he's doing, and practices it -- but I would only recommend his work to the kind of people who would benefit from a good swift karmic kick in the pants.

replies.title: Re: Jason Miller
replies.content:
Thanks JMG.

I understand why you've banned questions about evocation of spirits (it's potentially dangerous and shouldn't be playing fast and loose with dangerous entities and it is pretty easy to misuse etc), but I don't quite understand the angle about how it is better to develop the capacities within yourself to replace everything a spirit can do for you?

Do you mean instead of calling up a spirit and asking for a favour it's better to train yourself to be the kind of person who can achieve the same result without the spirit?

I just posted a separate reply which mentioned my experience trading in the financial markets - continuing with that analogy, would it be kind of like me saying, I can choose to simply observe and copy the trades that a famous or successful trader makes (some websites allow you to do this automatically and the trader you are copying gets a small cut of any profits you make), OR I can develop my own trading skills and market knowledge and execute trades based on my own skills and knowledge instead of copying someone else?

replies.title: Re: Jason Miller
replies.content:
Yes, that's part of it. You have the capacity to do for yourself anything that a spirit can do for you. You, just as you are now, are a perfect microcosm of the macrocosm, spanning the whole reach of reality from spirit to matter. You're like a prince who has forgotten his ancestry, sitting on a street corner in a distant village begging for loose change from passersby, when if you got up off your rump and walked to the capital and entered the palace you would be hailed as the lost heir to the kingdom. (Do you know the Gnostic Hymn of the Pearl? That's very nearly its exact plot.)

But there's more to it than that. Most of our problems as human beings are internal to us, and can't be fixed by changing circumstances. They can only be changed by recognizing how we're tripping over our own, er, feet, letting go of our self-defeating and self-immiserating habits and attitudes, and replacing them with habits and attitudes that lead to happiness and fulfillment. Change your thinking and your behavior, and you change your world; it really is as simple as that.

replies.title: Re: Jason Miller
replies.content:
I was online friends with a guy who was a member of Jason's "Gentelmen from Jupiter" for awhile. He had been mostly into dreamwork before, and a bit of Rosicrucian occult philosophy. When he started doing regular Jupiter workings (as Mr. Miller had advocated in books like Financial Sorcery [this could be another name for what they do on Wall St.]) he got a major promotion at his work and started making a lot more money. Subsequently he also had major stress events and some severe blowback. Him relating this to me was the last I heard from him.

On the other end of the spectrum I got quite a bit out of Josephine McCarthy's teachings about "wants and needs" and how as mages and occultists we need to differentiate between these. It's okay to have wants of course, but when we start doing lots of what she calls "results magic" to cater to those wants it can end up draining our magical pool of resources that might later give us our needs in times of need.

I think it is rude to badger spirits to do something for you that you could do yourself. And when you DIY you won't be in debt or tied to those spirits later in life. Meanwhile there are many positive being on the planes to work with in a more congenial way. I used to read Jason's blog for awhile and he had some interesting things on there occassionally, but I drifted when he started hammering more and more on how cool it was to work with demons. Sigh. Time to turn off that channel.

I'd also say there is a lot to be said for practicing the basic skills and doing the inner work necessary to prime you for making the most of opportunities that may present themselves when you align will, fate and destiny. Then you are doing the work yourself, and having needs & some wants met along the way. You earned it.

Justin Patrick Moore

replies.title: Re: Jason Miller
replies.content:
You know what I realized just reading your post?

I've recently restarted my quest (again) to become a professional trader in the financial markets and make a living from it. I've been doing it on and off for years, with a mixed record - initially some very bad trades and money lost, then improvement and consistent profits (but rarely trading) for a while and then more recently kind of a mixed bag with more active trading.

Overall from inception to now, I'm actually (very) profitable but being honest that wasn't due to the active trading, it was due to seeing potential early in some companies and buying them and hanging on for dear life (it's a different company but similar to buying NVidia 7-8 years ago).

Anyway, my point being - the best books about trading in the markets (a subject on which there is a very low signal to noise ratio - I've read only about 5 books, if that, which really fundamentally changed my views) which are written by successful traders, ALL emphasize that it is mostly a mental game, and you MUST train yourself to be detached and indifferent to whether the price goes up and if you can't reach that state, you will be too emotionally affected by the market to see the opportunities dispassionately.

You summarized pretty well in this sentence actually - it could almost be from one of those books (eg, a famous book about trading psychology called Trading in the Zone): "I'd also say there is a lot to be said for practicing the basic skills and doing the inner work necessary to prime you for making the most of opportunities that may present themselves when you align will, fate and destiny. Then you are doing the work yourself, and having needs & some wants met along the way. You earned it."

replies.title: Re: Evolutionary Astrology
replies.content:
I have an autism spectrum syndrome (more so when I was younger), and evolutionary psychology, and especially the intersection of game theory with evolutionary psychology, put a ton of order to some things about human nature that I otherwise had to treat as a grab bag of disconnected observations (having fangs if I ignored them) and/or get totally blindsided by.

It had been really frustrating and painful when some of the things about human nature that I had to go along with ran counter to the values that everyone expressed, and everyone had this invisible consensus pulling their strings behind the scenes to coordinate them to get mad at me when I tried to go along with people's explicitly stated values, unconditioned by the information from their social instincts about how some policies would impose huge costs on people in ways they didn't know how to articulate an explicit conceptual description of. Usually these costs would involve precedents: the evolutionary psychology concept of "welfare tradeoff ratios" implied by a given interaction, either because of Trivers's coefficient of relatedness or as a negotiated outcome where different parties care different amounts about a contested resource and can impose different costs or withhold different benefits, is critical for really understanding huge amounts of things, if you're as relatively autistic as I was.

I guess the standard pop-culture-osmosis/popularly-received-wisdom version of evolutionary psychology doesn't really emphasize welfare tradeoff ratios, the coefficient of relatedness (and the "rough and ready heuristics" model of how brains estimate it, which allow adoption to be a thing), the idea that humans execute adaptations rather than have an explicitly cognized concept of fitness to maximize, and Robert Trivers's concept of adaptive self-deception -- which has a place in evolutionary psychology roughly equivalent to the place of the concept of the fall of man in Abrahamic religion, but scientifically hanging together consiliently in a more intelligible way. I don't know why.

There's this famous saying about now nothing in biology makes sense except in light of evolution. Psychology is part of biology, so if there was nothing to be understood about psychology from evolution, that would be very strange. The founding strain of evolutionary psychology starting from Tooby and Cosmides tried hard using what seemed to me to be a good scientific strategy and attitude -- one that erred on the side of methodological caution, even -- so if they didn't achieve anything valuable in the end, then that would imply that the problem of investigating the relation between psychology and evolution is a lot harder than one would mostly have ever thought to expect.

I think most people have baked-in instincts that are invisibly feeding them hints about human nature and motivations all the time, that create this illusion for them as though the stuff that evolutionary psychology articulates was vacuous because "obviously thus-and-such behavioral peculiarity is exactly what you'd expect". (To whom, coming from Greek-Christian philosophical predispositions about what kinds of things minds would be, would it have ever occurred to expect that sex would have such a huge pile of motivations and instinctive strategies around it, as opposed to being some sort of focus of sober instrumental reasoning?) It's hard to really appreciate unless you're starting from some weird point of view that removes stuff we take for granted, like a strict "blank slate" assumption about how learning and habit-formation works (like those progressive parents who are perplexed when their children strongly show gender-typical differences in interests and activities despite gender-neutral environmental cues), or some kind of cultivated philosophical detachment like Marcus Aurelius where everything has the meaning stripped out of it that would trigger automatic reactions, or an autism-spectrum condition.

Obligatory occultism topic-contact: If someone wanted to purse a fusion of emotional/instinctinve/intuitive/social thinking with conceptual/mechanical/quantitative thinking, I think they would benefit greatly from giving their conceptual thinking correct conceptual frames and languages for what the emotional thinking was doing. You could get that some other way, but evolutionary psychology seems like it would be a bad trade-off to pass up, in that connection. There's also some cosmological awareness benefit from being able to see, in outline, the mechanisms connecting broader processes of historical decision-like processes made by gene-frequencies and small-band social dynamics to narrower processes of individual cognition and instinctive motivation -- thus way without, thus other but structurally-related way within.

comments.title: Drawing Celtic Knots

I've stumbled across another method for drawing Celtic knots and wanted to call it to the attention of the cord and knot crowd. Here is a post I made on my dreamwidth account on the method: https://kylec.dreamwidth.org/50000.html

The short version is that you can use geometrical figures as the basis for designing the knots instead of a grid. I also show how to use the method in reverse to take a cool knot picture and figure out what geometrical figures are the basis for it.

On another note, I had been very excited about cord and knot work and was going ham after it, so I decided to slow down and cast a divination as to whether cord magic was something I should pursue. I will still work on my knot-tying for practical purposes, and maybe draw a thing or two, but beyond that I think I have to turn it over to other interested parties. Good luck to yall!

comments.title: Q
comments.content:
Greetings everyone, a few questions.

1) I am slowly losing trust that astrology actually is real. I noticed that as my interest in astrology dwindled, the results seem to stop working (in transits, relationship charts etc). It made me think that perhaps there's some perceptual - mind related reason for why it seems like it works when you accept it, but doesn't otherwise.

I saw a recent thread on twitter where they tried to run an experiment on 152 astrologers https://x.com/SpencrGreenberg/status/1822663687145972105 and found zero predictive ability. Now, perhaps the tests weren't well designed, but it still remarkable that there was zero predictive ability.

Here's an account of an astrologer who after decades gave up astrology after a series of events: https://bogardiner.wordpress.com/2015/03/08/a-professional-astrologist-realizes-astrology-isnt-real/

Yet, I find horary to work. I'm not sure if horary is simply using astrology's symbols and divination is a completely separate thing.

So, I would like to ask people to steelman astrology and offer some explanations and thoughts for what's going on. Is astrology some really advanced perceptual glitch so to speak?

2) Related somewhat, I am trying to figure out the symbolic structure of reality. It seems like narratives are real "thing" so to speak, and they have symbolic correspondences with the rest of reality, in a way where our lives harmonise with a core set of symbols. I am not sure if I am riffing Jung, but it seems to be there's only a core set of symbols and our lives/stories are derivatives of these core images/stories, in some sort of fractal pattern. We are in someways, always inside a "story". It would also imply that there's somewhat of an overriding narrative or symbol for humanity itself.

Master Greer, could you share your thoughts on this and recommend me some reading material?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
1) Are you at all familiar with the theory of interpersonal games, from the school of psychology called Transactional Analysis? Eric Berne's book Games People Play is a good intro. I mention this because what you've done here is invite people to play one of those games. It's called "Prove It To Me," and it's a really good way to waste time if that's what you want to do. The real (and covert) rule is that no matter what evidence is presented to you, it's not enough, so around and around it goes.

Me, I have plenty of other things to occupy my time, so I'll pass. Whether or not you find astrology believable, or useful, or true is ultimately none of my business, you know, nor does it affect my experience with astrology. Have a good time with the game!

2) Terry Pratchett somewhere has some writings about a "theory of narrative causality," which you might find interesting. Of course you can also wade into Jung -- Man and Symbol is a good place to start.

replies.title: Re: Q

Thanks on 2!

replies.content:

(2) Speaking just for myself, neither desire, belief nor perception have narrative structures. I have never been able to think (or do much of anything mentally) in sentences and propositions. comments.title: spiritual corruption?
comments.content:
If I remember correctly, I read here some time ago about the idea that people practicing an exercise/working 'laid down tracks in space' so to speak, and that could be beneficial for those following the path later.

As such, I've been wondering if it is possible for a system/metaphor/egregor to be corrupted because the 'tracks in space' change direction because of the way practice is done - similar to people becoming what they fight against, but in the context of thinking one is doing one thing but actually doing something else.
A bit like the idea of more than one being/entity representing Jesus - multiple 'Jesi' and not all the same at all.

If a group can have an egregor that develops in a disharmonious way, I've been thinking/wondering about how this might manifest and if it may be possible for a 'corruption' to lay down tracks in space that users of the metaphor might inadvertently enter a path they are not expecting.

Think of the Orwellian use of language seen in recent years - hijacking of language that inhibits communication and thought, but applied to spiritual practices.
Obviously one imagines this could take a great deal of time and effort to effect a shift in direction, but those two factors are not an impossible obstacle and what started as one thing could become something else.
Granted that time, place, circumstances and people all play a part and symbols have multiple uses around the world.

On the grand scale of things it seems one may be drawn to the heavenly or devilish (or just blow about in the wind), and this all manifests in different ways, and those who choose the animalistic and devilish may eventually shift direction in good time, but meanwhile, mischievous tendencies seem either ascendant or shouting a last hurrah.
Looking at what people do and how they actually behave and live seems to offer its own warning, and my thinking is that spiritual hygiene of one form or another is extra critical just now and I've been giving some thought to methods of hygiene that extend beyond the obvious.

Clearly some things are best kept quiet, but for some reason I've been wondering about whether an entire system/metaphor could become either unstable, a cess-pit, unusable, or at the very least a place to tread with extreme care.

Granted that treading with care seems like it should be default in the current world; and even more so re the occult, but this question has been trundling around in my head for a while now - an unvoiced 'awareness' that seems to be saying 'don't use that'. A sort of intuition but I am not sure 'what' I am 'intuiting'.

It may be something or nothing, but any thoughts to chew-on much appreciated.

Thank you.

You have to shut down the lodge, disperse the members, wait a while, and then start a new working with new people and new rituals.

comments.title: The Fae & The Western Mystery Tradition
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

Can you suggest a good place / places to start with regard to understanding The Fae and how it fits with The Western Mystery Tradition?

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: The Fae & The Western Mystery Tradition
replies.content:
Sure. I would start with Rev. Robert Kirk's The Secret Commonwealth of Elves, Fauns, and Fairies, originally published in 1691, which you can download here:

https://archive.org/details/Kirk1893CommonwealthElves/page/n1/mode/2up

Kirk was familiar with the occultism of his time and also, as a Scottish clergyman, with the faery lore of his own countryside. Folklore claims to this day that he was taken by the faeries in revenge for spilling their secrets. After that, I recommend the books of Katharine Briggs, especially The Anatomy of Puck and The Vanishing People, and then Jacques Vallee's Passport to Magonia.

Yes, I know, none of these works are actually part of the literature of the Western mystery tradition; one is a work of scholarship by a 17th century Clergyman with Rosicrucian leanings, the next two are by a folklore scholar, and the last is by a UFO investigator. They'll still give you a much better grasp of the tradition than anything yet produced in Western esoteric literature as such.

replies.title: Re: The Fae & The Western Mystery Tradition
replies.content: Thank you!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Giambattista Vico wrote about this three hundred years ago in the pages of The New Science. When a civilization is going to bits, one of the common features is exactly this kind of collective mental fugue. He called it "the barbarism of reflection" -- the point at which reason parts company with reality, and one plus one equals whatever it's politically convenient for your side to claim it is. The result is the collapse of communication and thought, making it impossible for the society to deal with its problems or even continue to provide basic necessities. The upside, as Vico also points out, is that as long as this is mostly concentrated in the ruling class, it can be solved by the self-inflicted implosion of that class and its replacement from below -- or from abroad.

replies.title: Re: Beta blockers, Mysticism, amd magic

replies.title: Re: SoP: Invoke first and banish later

I was recently reading Fortune's, "Moon Magic", and in a passage she curses "our modern Moloch" (page 222) I couldn't find any further mention of this in the book, nor in the discussions on the main Ecosophian page, so I figured I'd ask about it here. replies.title: Re: Embarassed alumna
replies.content:
1) Thank you for this. That makes sense.

2) Moloch or Molech is an Old Testament slander: a Canaanite god who was called Melekh, "the King" (you didn't speak the true name of a god lightly in those days). The Hebrews wrote it with the vowel points of bosheth, "shame," as a nasty pun. They also insisted that the Canaanites sacrificed children to him, which may have been true or may have been a blood libel of the usual kind. As a metaphor in English, it stands for anything to which human lives are sacrificed. Fortune uses it as a metaphor here for Victorian sexual morality. She saw all too clearly the horrific amount of emotional and psychological misery it caused.

I've looked at online porn since I was 13 (as have many others in my cohort or younger). I wouldn't say I was ever addicted, but I've seen more than my fair share. More recently, I've cut back on it, although it's still an on & off habit, and I've participated in ascetic fasts like NNN (and made it the whole month of November!).

replies.title: Re: xxx
replies.content:
Okay, first of all there's nothing new about the current distribution of sexual energies. Pornography was a huge industry in Roman times, and again from the Renaissance on; it's indicative that one of the very first things that happened when photography was invented was that people started taking pictures of women with their clothes off. Sex was also much more readily available in other eras than it is now. Did you know that one of the reforms for which Solon, the great legislator of Athens, was famous was establishing brothels run by the city government, with fixed, inexpensive prices, so that young men could get their rocks off any time they wanted without making trouble? James Davidson's book Courtesans and Fishcakes is worth reading in this context.

The astral effects of pornography, mind you, aren't good; the more you consume, the more any sexual arousal will immediately be pulled down to the crassest and grubbiest level, and gradually numb the sexual senses, so that more and more stimulation is necessary to get any reaction at all. comments.title: 72 hour rule question
comments.content:
Hello JMG

My auntie recently died. I asked my cousin to have the funeral home wait for at least 72 hours before cremating her and my cousin was fine with this. Later he asked me why this was important and I didn't have a really good answer for it. Can you say a bit about where this lore hails from? Is this from a specific tradition? Can I read about it somewhere?

Thank you very much for hosting this space (as well as the other spaces, too). I learn so much from you and from everyone else who participates here.

MJ from MA

replies.title: Re: 72 hour rule question
replies.content: I think Dion Fortune references it in Through the Gates of Death, but she's far from the only one. It's a general bit of occult lore, and the reason for it is that it takes time for the subtle bodies to finish detaching completely from the material body at death. The time varies, but it's pretty much always completed by the 72-hour mark; if you cremate the body before then, and the subtle bodies aren't completely detached, the soul will feel some pain from the burning. It's not something that causes lasting harm but it can be traumatic.

comments.title: Evolution through the Kingdoms of Nature and Beyond
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Anthroposophy as I understand it posits seven kingdoms of nature above the elemental levels/kingdoms in our "scheme", an angelic hierarchy model, with human beings as the 4th kingdom, graduating (on the subsequent 5th globe, Jupiter, on the etheric plane) to Angels; in turn graduating (on the subsquent 6th globe, Venus, on the astral plane) to Archangels; in turn graduating (on the subsequent 7th globe, Vulcan, on the mentsl plane) to Archai or Potentates.

In the Theosophical school, as I understand it, humans evolve/graduate to the 5th kingdom or "Hierarchy of Masters" or "Kingdom of Souls", from which they can take seven different "paths" at least within the current globe-scheme, including the path of the Bodhisattva, the path of a Solar Logos, the path of Absolute Sonship, etc. So there comes a level of "initiation" (9th initiation?) where the scheme spilts off as a point of departure for divergent paths. I'm wondering what the Order of the Golden Dawn (which I'm not very familiar with) has to say about our evolution through the kindgoms of nature, and the trajectory of "higher evolution"? What is our telos/purpose if anything? What are your personal beliefs on this?

As an ancillary (merely hypothetical) question, suppose human (and other sentient) life on earth was terminated in the near future through nuclear destruction/devastation. How to your mind would this affect the evolutionary plan/trajectory? Also, what effect might it have on all those already-departed/discarnate souls, since they too would have played a role while on earth in the "preparatory work" for such an ominous event.

Many thanks for your thoughts!

replies.title: Re: Evolution through the Kingdoms of Nature and Beyond
replies.content:
1) The Golden Dawn didn't have teachings about that. It was focused very intensively on practice, and those who wanted to discuss the theory of spiritual evolution could go hang out with the Theosophists, Anthroposophists, et al. My personal opinion is that material incarnation is the prenatal condition of the soul; in effect, in spiritual terms, we haven't even been born yet, and we're roughly as capable of understanding the telos of our souls as an unborn infant is capable of understanding the doctorate degree it'll earn thirty years or so after birth.

2) If it happens, it will be because it is allowed to happen by the divine and angelic powers that govern evolution on this earth. Presumably those who are ready to deal with disembodied existence will do so, and the rest will have to wait until a new sentient species evolves -- which probably won't take too long, since all that radiation will speed up the rate of mutation and thus give natural selection much more to work with. A million years is an eyeblink in geological time, remember...

replies.title: Re: Evolution through the Kingdoms of Nature and Beyond
replies.content:
Regarding the first point, I like the Golden Dawn's emphasis on practice (or "praxis", if one is a Marxist!). Gandhi once said that "Theosophy is Hinduism in theory; Hinduism is theosophy in practice." Having said that, do you see any merit in studying these theories (e.g. the possible benefit of mental development, creation of lofty thought forms that could precipiate to the lower planes, knowledge that may be helpful in the discarnate worlds, etc.), or is it a waste of time and an instance of bypassing lived reality for unknown/unknowable abstractions and projections? Furthermore, do you personally find any glimpses of truth or knowledge there?

On the second point, John Lennon was once asked in an interview (1980) about how he reconciled his belief in human consciousness evolution with the looming threat of nuclear catastrophe and the possibility of the elimination of all life on earth. He responded that even after nuclear destruction, life goes on. It doesn't end. If you're still alive, you must deal with being alive on earth and rebuilding; if you're "dead" you have to deal with life in the "beyond". Either way life continues unabated.

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Evolution through the Kingdoms of Nature and Beyond
replies.content:
1) Yes, there's a point to occult study alongside occult practice. I prefer Dion Fortune's The Cosmic Doctrine, which covers the same ground more concisely, to either Blavatsky's or Steiner's version, but learning to think in terms of any such system is good mental training.

2) Exactly.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not everyone has such experiences with love, you know. The fact that you're having them suggests several things to me. The first is that, as you've said, you had bad parenting and came out of it with a desperate need for approval and affection from others. Most people who have that sort of emotional damage also have various self-defeating habits that guarantee that their childhood experiences of rejection will be repeated in adult life, as "infatuations and completely losing it" would suggest. Here again, though, what you're going through now -- wretched though it is -- is only one possible experience of love. Some people fall in love and really do stay in love until death parts them; that's the situation I'm dealing with now, as you may have heard. Some people have still other experiences. It's a complicated realm of human interaction.

I'm going to make a specific suggestion, which you are of course free to ignore. I suggest, if you haven't done this already, you consider working through the Octagon Society exercises -- this is one of the initiatory systems I received from my teacher John Gilbert. comments.title: The Process of Dying
comments.content:
Dear J.M. Greer

I'm curious about any teachings in your own writings, or in the Western traditions generally, about the elemental and subtle body aspects of stages of the death process. In particular, anything comparable, parallel, or similar to the following: in Vajrayana Highest Yoga Tantra the process of death and dissolution of consciousness is presented in eight stages (except in Kalachakra, where it's divided into ten): the successive dissolution of Earth, Water, Fire, and Air, which corresponds respectively to a counterclockwise circuit of the directional attributions of these elements in Tantra, North, West, South, East; followed by the White bindu in the crown, then the Red bindu in the pelvic center, which descend and ascend toward the heart center as they dissolve; the final Black dissolution in the heart center; and then the vision of Clear Light luminosity, or primordial light. Each of these stages is accompanied by signs or appearances, and they are worked with in many yogic practices. Tantric adepts of these practices are known to enter the meditative state of tukdam after clinical death, which is why people are advised to leave the body of the yogi alone for at least three days after apparent death.
The order of the sequence of Elements followed by White, Red, and Black makes me suspect there may be something similar in Western alchemy, or some other Western traditions. I would be grateful for any comments or suggestions you may have.

Regards,

Josh Rout

replies.title: Re: The Process of Dying
replies.content: I don't know of anything so detailed. There's simply a recognition of the shedding of the material body, then the etheric body, and then the dissolution of the astral body. You're familiar with Dion Fortune's Through the Gates of Death, right? That's the most detailed description I know of.

replies.title: Re: The Process of Dying
replies.content: Very hastily, I think there's material in various forms in Neoplatonic/Hermetic/Gnostic traditions, largely having to do with ascent through the planetary spheres, and the shedding of planetary bodies or sheaths along the way. Some of this seems to derive from Egyptian sources. At the other end, there's the Swedenborgian/Spiritualist current. But it's a big subject.

replies.title: Re: The Process of Dying
replies.content: I've seen that, but I haven't seen it correlated with the ordinary process of death -- it's specific to liberation from incarnation.

As always, thank you for Magic Mondays and your sage advice.

replies.title: Re: Untrue & Unfaithful Pathworking Guide

comments.title: Shuffling Coelbren

Trying to shuffle normally (like a deck of cards) ends up with mine all turned every sort of which way. replies.title: Re: Shuffling Coelbren
replies.content: I keep mine in a cloth bag, shake it, and then pull them out one at a time by one end.
